chunk_summary: Guidance on meal frequency, nutrient density, and age-specific dietary counseling for children 6–23 months, school-age children, and adolescents to address chronic undernutrition and stunting.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific meal frequency and nutrient density recommendations for children and adolescents.
Learn strategies to enhance energy and protein intake in low-appetite children.
Identify dietary practices to support oral-motor development and school performance.
Recognize the role of structured meals and snacks in appetite regulation and nutrient adequacy.
key_facts: Breastfed infants aged 6–8 months need 2–3 meals/day; 9–23 months need 3–4 meals/day with snacks as needed.
Non-breastfed children require more meals plus milk feeds to meet nutritional needs.
Protein should be included in every main meal and at least one snack for school-age children and adolescents.
Energy density can be increased by adding oil/ghee to khichdi or dal, nut paste to porridge, or curd/egg to meals.
Tea and biscuits should be replaced with milk and nutrient-dense snacks to avoid empty calories and nutrient displacement.
Choking-risk precautions are essential for young children when introducing nuts or hard foods.
reference_quote: Breastfed infants typically need 2–3 meals per day at 6–8 months and 3–4 meals per day at 9–23 months, with additional nutritious snacks as needed. (Source: WHO complementary feeding guideline, 2023)
document_key_topics: Meal frequency and nutrient density for children 6–23 months
Dietary strategies for school-age children and adolescents
Energy and protein adequacy in low-appetite children
Choking-risk precautions and structured meal planning
